steps.title: Find the key contrast in the data
steps.content:
Three-host parasites (which depend on at least two vertebrate species) showed declining abundance as sea temperatures rose.

Two-host parasites (which depend on only one vertebrate species) remained stable.

The passage cites this difference to suggest something about how parasite vulnerability is related to the number of vertebrate hosts they require.

steps.title: Match the explanation to the choices
steps.content:
warming-induced population changes among Puget Sound vertebrates may have suppressed some parasite populations, but there were no changes among the eight studied host species that affected two-host parasite abundance. explains the observed pattern: warming-induced population changes among Puget Sound vertebrates may have suppressed some three-host parasite populations, but among the eight studied fish species specifically, there were no changes that affected two-host parasite abundance.

The other choices either reverse the observed effect, contradict the passage's claim that vertebrates are vulnerable to climate change, or assert a relationship opposite to the data (D—parasites with fewer hosts were less sensitive, not more).

The pattern in the data makes sense if parasites with more host dependencies are more vulnerable because warming can disrupt any of their multiple required vertebrate hosts.

That matches the correct answer — C is correct.
